source.seedLabel: Michael Kiernan (CRO, Nextdoor) on Human+Agentic GTM, the operator-first CRO path, and new monetization beyond ads
topics: CRM data foundation as AI ceiling

topics.title: Human + Agentic, not Just Agentic
topics.angle: The framing you pick for AI transformation tells your team whether they're being augmented or replaced. 'Agentic GTM' bodifies the org. 'Human + Agentic GTM' brings the team along. The word 'plus' is the change-management strategy, not a marketing nuance.
topics.sourceQuote: We've all as a business, we've got to go from maybe being agentic first to agentic enabled to agentic native. To position it like we're going to automate and bodify the organization. I actually don't think that's the right way to get the team on board with that transformation.

topics.title: The Agentic-Per-Segment Spectrum
topics.angle: You don't pick ONE AI strategy for sales. You pick three: enterprise (AI makes the rep sharper, never owns the room), mid-market (AI-enabled, human last), SMB (agent owns the outreach, human reviews and hits send). Where you apply AI is inversely proportional to ACV.
topics.sourceQuote: It's a spectrum as you work more upmarket, there's an inverse relationship to how much AI you're putting into the customer facing portion of the process. Agentic is powering the sales experience. It is not owning the relationship or the room or the close, the agent's there to make the rep look good, to make the rep sharper.

topics.title: Accountability Doesn't Bot
topics.angle: You can automate the workflow. You cannot automate the accountability. Buyers want to know a human is on the hook when a real deal is on the line. The bigger the deal, the more this matters. This is why fully-automated outbound is turning enterprise buyers off, not converting them.
topics.sourceQuote: It's very difficult to put the accountability onto AI, onto an agent, especially if you're closing a bigger deal. I want to know that someone's on the hook for that deal. The volume's 10X for me. It's not that great. It's like turning me off to this entire experience.

topics.title: The AI Pendulum: Centralized to Wild West to Middle
topics.angle: Most companies are now on their third AI org model in 18 months. Centralized AI team to let-every-team-go-rogue to a $20k surprise bill to guardrails in the middle. The lesson: skip the first two phases. Start with cost guardrails AND federated ownership on day one.
topics.sourceQuote: We pendulum started kind of way out to the left, then it swung way out to the right, and now it feels like it's starting to settle a little bit more in the middle. Some kind of cost and tools-based guardrails, but still allowing the individual teams and process owners to have that kind of final say.

topics.title: The $20k Surprise Bill
topics.angle: The first sign your AI experimentation is real isn't a productivity dashboard. It's an invoice nobody expected. One team running Claude in the background. Twenty grand a month. The bill is a leading indicator that you need guardrails, not a reason to shut it down.
topics.sourceQuote: Then the bill came, right? Someone in IT was like, whoa, like someone over there spent like $20,000 in a month because they were doing a bunch of stuff with cowork in the background and they probably didn't totally understand the efficiency of it.

topics.title: Your CRM Quality Is Now Your AI Ceiling
topics.angle: Most companies are about to discover that their data foundation, not their model choice, is the limit on AI productivity. Nextdoor crawled through a hard Salesforce-Databricks integration BEFORE the AI moment, and now non-data-scientists are running like data scientists. Skip the crawl, hit the ceiling.
topics.sourceQuote: We learned the hard way through our Salesforce integration that we had a lot of tough ground we had to crawl to get our CRM in a really great spot. But now that I feel like it is, we're realizing that the way Salesforce and Databricks talk to our different AI tools have made the process really easy for people like me, not a data scientist.

topics.title: The Operator-First CRO Path
topics.angle: The fastest-growing path to CRO right now isn't quota-carrying AE to sales manager. It's RevOps/SE to ops leader to sales-comfortable executive. The new CRO is operationally fluent first and a closer second. Most RevOps leaders underestimate how short the runway actually is.
topics.sourceQuote: The more I meet other executives and leaders in our industry, the more I realize they're not just sales leaders, they're incredibly operational. Some of them have management consulting backgrounds. My path was more of an ops guy that got comfortable in sales.

topics.title: Don't Run From RevOps, Stack These 3 Experiences
topics.angle: To the RevOps leader who wants to be CRO: don't jump out and grab an AE bag for the optics. Pin-seek these three career assignments instead, a new line of business, an international expansion, a painful re-org. That's how you see the whole elephant.
topics.sourceQuote: I would be absolutely pin-seeking any opportunity you can to work on a new line of business, work on international expansion, work on, sometimes they're painful, but work on a re-org. And all of that is seeing the whole elephant.

topics.title: You Don't Need a Bag, You Need the Pressure
topics.angle: The advice carry a bag to be a real exec misses the point. What you actually need is a stretch of years where a meaningful slice of your comp is tied to revenue. Kiernan structured it as 30%+ since his first ops role. The pressure is the lesson, not the title on the comp plan.
topics.sourceQuote: Since that turn job, I have always had 30% or more of my compensation tied to revenue outcomes. And so I feel like I have always felt the pressure of contributing to the top line. I don't think owning a number so that you can say you own the number is the point.

topics.title: The 25/25/25/25 CRO Calendar
topics.angle: Kiernan color-codes his calendar in 4 colors and audits it weekly. Quarter for core revenue. Quarter for new monetization. Quarter for the agentic transformation. Quarter for people. If any block hasn't shown up in two weeks, that's the audit signal, not a guess.
topics.sourceQuote: I color code my calendar this way. I literally stare at it. You want it to be roughly speaking like a quarter blue, a quarter red, a quarter green, a quarter yellow. That is a mechanism to keep you on track. You could use it as a little bit of a self-audit when maybe you've had a couple weeks where you felt like you haven't interacted with a customer.

topics.title: Hire People Better Than You, Even Into Your Own Org
topics.angle: The unlock for a CRO trying to redistribute time isn't outsourcing or AI. It's hiring complementary talent INTO your existing org that's better than you at the part you're trying to step back from. Kiernan went from all my time on core revenue to a quarter of my time by hiring one VP.
topics.sourceQuote: It was a good moment for me to like remember like you can actually hire people better than you, even if they're in your org, and they take things so much further. And so for me, focusing on core revenue, which is our ads business, was nearly all of my time last year. Right now, this year, I'm trying to make that be about a quarter of my time.

topics.title: Shared Priorities Map: The Forgotten Exec Exercise
topics.angle: Most exec teams set individual OKRs and never check the dependency graph between them. Nextdoor's CEO reframed each leader's priorities as either solo or SHARED with another C-suite peer. Kiernan can't agentify GTM without Craig (Product). That coupling is the priority, not either person's wishlist.
topics.sourceQuote: He led a conversation with us, a series of conversations, where we really focused on longer term priorities. It was for us across the management team to understand where there was either a shared priority with someone else on the team. Kiernan wants to agentify his team great but he can't do that without buy-in from Craig who runs our product organization.

topics.title: The Speed-to-Market AI Scorecard
topics.angle: Stop asking is the AI working? Start tracking these six numbers: meeting volume per rep, pipeline stage conversion, client response times, customer adoption of recommendations, revenue per head, and cost utilization. If those aren't moving in 90 days, your AI isn't working, your bill is just growing.
topics.sourceQuote: Speed to market, I think we'll see that. In meeting volume. You'll start to see that in conversion rates between pipeline stages. We'll see it in our outbound for sure. We'll see it in our revenue per head will go up. All the efficiency metrics you look for when you're evaluating a public company's cost utilization.

topics.title: Selling Never Ends in Ad/Platform Businesses
topics.angle: SaaS reps close, then hand to CS. Ad/platform reps never hand off, they keep selling the next budget cycle, the tentpole event, the new format. If you're applying a SaaS GTM playbook to an ad/platform/marketplace business, you've already built the wrong org chart.
topics.sourceQuote: The selling never ends. It's very much unlike maybe SaaS, where once the rep gets a contract or a license over the line, they tend to hand it to a customer success or account management team. That's not our business model.

topics.title: Why Ad-Model Companies Don't Run SDR
topics.angle: Nextdoor has no SDR layer. The hand-off kills the relationship the rep needs to keep selling. Senior reps prospect themselves, and AI is finally making senior reps comfortable doing the prospecting work they hated. The SDR org isn't dying because of AI; it's dying because it never fit some business models in the first place.
topics.sourceQuote: We don't have an SDR model. We don't love the handoff. You'll see it in some of our really senior season strategic reps. You'll see them start to move faster on the prospecting side of things when historically, I think the older you get, the less you like to do that kind of work.

topics.title: Be Slower on AI in New Product Lines
topics.angle: Counterintuitive: the place to be MOST cautious with agentic isn't enterprise. It's new monetization. New product needs nuanced customer feedback you can't get from a survey or agent. Move fast on AI in mature offerings. Move slowly on AI in zero-to-one. Inverted from how most teams are sequencing it.
topics.sourceQuote: As you move to lower ACV customers for newer product experiences, we need to be more careful there. You're putting a new product in market. You need to learn from that. The fastest way to learn from customers experiencing a new product is to talk to them.

topics.title: Every Role Should Live in Gong
topics.angle: Finance shouldn't approve a comp plan without listening to calls. RevOps shouldn't redesign territories without listening to calls. The fastest way to STOP getting promoted into senior business roles is to grow distant from raw customer conversations. Pay attention to what customers are saying, whether it's your job or not.
topics.sourceQuote: Whether you're the one interacting with the customer or not, pay attention to what customers are saying. That is incredibly important. I think everyone in my organization needs to be digging into what's going on in Gong because I think there's some real gold in there. That's actually how you don't get a CRO job.

topics.title: From Agentic-First to Agentic-Enabled to Agentic-Native
topics.angle: There's a 3-stage maturity curve for AI in a business, and most companies confuse stage 1 for stage 3. Agentic-first equals experiments. Agentic-enabled equals real workflows assisted. Agentic-native equals the org couldn't function without it. Knowing which stage you're at changes what you optimize for next quarter.
topics.sourceQuote: We've all as a business, we've got to go from maybe being agentic first to agentic enabled to agentic native. And we've got that means we've got to bring people through that.

topics.title: The New Function Question for AI
topics.angle: Do you spin up a new department to drive AI transformation, or let existing functions own it? Depends on company structure. Functional orgs let functions own it. GM/line-of-business orgs let GMs own it. Weak data foundation, you NEED a centralized transformation group. Your structure picks for you.
topics.sourceQuote: I think it totally depends on the company. Nextdoor is mostly functionally structured. I do think kind of lends itself down this path of we'll let the functions own their processes. I think some companies are structured around lines of business or in a GM structure.

topics.title: State University Data Scientists
topics.angle: The most undersold benefit of mature data infra plus AI tools: regular operators with no Stanford PhD can now do data scientist work on their own. Kiernan calls himself a state university data scientist and is shipping. The bottleneck on analytics talent just collapsed for companies who put in the foundational work.
topics.sourceQuote: The way Salesforce and Databricks talk to our different AI tools have made the process really easy for people like me, not a data scientist, state university. I am my own data scientist and I'm pretty successful at it because the underlying foundation actually was in a pretty healthy spot.

topics.title: Beyond Ads: Monetizing the Neighborhood Graph
topics.angle: The most interesting monetization moves don't come from scaling the core revenue engine. They come from recognizing organic activity already happening (someone needs a plumber, someone has 100 cookies to sell) and putting a thoughtful business model behind it. The platform companies that win next decade will monetize 3-5 things, not one.
topics.sourceQuote: There are other things that happen on Nextdoor that are transactions or potentially commercial in nature that aren't necessarily ad models. We have people every day posting saying like, hey, I need a plumber. We're thinking about different ways to help the neighbor find their plumber faster, to help the plumber find a new customer faster.

topics.title: Agentic Use Cases Map by Sales Motion
topics.angle: A specific 1-page matrix every CRO should build. For each segment (Enterprise/Mid-market/SMB), what does AI DO vs what does the human DO. Enterprise: AI preps, human owns. Mid-market: AI accelerates, human approves. SMB: AI executes, human reviews. Without this map, every team will reinvent the wheel.
topics.sourceQuote: Where does agentic come into play for our enterprise approach? It's doing things like research before meetings, meeting prep, proposal drafts. Mid-market that's where we got to start thinking about scale. SMB, that's where you really do get more comfortable allowing the agent to take over the outreach, lead routing, follow-up prompts for renewal.

topics.title: The Curiosity Tax
topics.angle: When you tell teams go experiment with AI, you're choosing to pay a curiosity tax, surprise bills, redundant tools, paid subscriptions nobody approved. Most leaders try to skip the tax and end up with a frozen org instead. The tax IS the org-wide learning. Pay it on purpose.
topics.sourceQuote: People are jumping in, people are trying to use different tools. People are going rogue and paying for things they probably shouldn't be paying for and integrating things they probably shouldn't be integrating, but whatever. It's good for the company for that level of curiosity. Let the employee base do their thing.

topics.title: Avoid the Big Mistakes, Not Every Mistake
topics.angle: On AI cost paranoia: the standard isn't spend zero. It isn't even show ROI. It's avoid the catastrophic mistakes. The trick is to set a low floor on losses, not a high bar on wins. Most teams over-engineer ROI tracking and under-engineer guardrails against the $100k whoopsie.
topics.sourceQuote: I'm so paranoid around our company or people, especially on my team spending too much on the tools, but it's not as bad as we thought it was going to be. The trick there is to avoid the big mistakes is the way I kind of view it.

topics.title: The Zero-to-One Operator Skill
topics.angle: Kiernan's whole career is one pattern: spot a thing that needs to go from zero-to-one, build the spreadsheet, set the cadence, draw the Gantt chart, win. It's not a sales skill, an ops skill, or a product skill, it's a separate operating skill, and it's the most undervalued capability in modern CRO hiring.
topics.sourceQuote: At the core is always focusing on what needs to go from zero to one and trying to take it there. And I think that always starts with trying to be organized, looking at the data, focusing on the communication cadence. And so when I say I'm an operational leader, that's what I mean.

topics.title: The Inbound Quality Cliff
topics.angle: Inbound volume for sales tools is up 10x. Inbound quality is off a cliff. The buyers you most want, operating CROs evaluating sales tools, are now actively turned OFF by automated outreach. Every founder selling AI to GTM teams is competing in a market they themselves are sick of.
topics.sourceQuote: I'd be willing to bet over the past six months to a year, you have gotten a ton of inbound to you as a potential buyer. And like that inbound, it's like super low quality. The volume's like 10X for me. As someone on the other side of that, in particular for me, I'm getting inbound for sales tools, training programs. It's not that great. It's like turning me off to this entire experience.

topics.title: Science Fair Strategy for New Revenue Lines
topics.angle: How a public-company CRO talks about exploring new revenue: it feels like a science fair experiment. Lots of ideas. Wide range from on-brand to wild. Wide range from easy to impossible. The honesty about that posture, and protecting time for it, is what separates leaders who find next-gen monetization from those who only defend the core.
topics.sourceQuote: What are we doing next in monetization? Right now it feels like a little bit like a science fair experiment, to be candid. There's way more ideas on the table than we can deliver. Pretty broad range of both upside and like super upside of are they going to be easy or impossible? Are they totally on brand or are they a little bit out there.

topics.title: The Data Moat Becomes a Monetization Asset
topics.angle: For platforms with real first-party data (Nextdoor's 110M verified neighbors), the data moat itself becomes a new revenue line, not by selling data, but by carefully designing on-platform and off-platform models that monetize the graph. Most companies have less data leverage than they think; the ones with real moats are sitting on un-shipped revenue.
topics.sourceQuote: We've got 110 million people using Nextdoor all the time. We're in one in three homes. We work with partners who can also tell us that. Mike Kiernan at X116 Pacific Street lives in a brownstone and it definitely doesn't have a pool. We are thinking about different ways where our neighborhood graph becomes a monetization asset on platform or off platform too.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The Agentic-Per-Segment Spectrum
posts.postText:
**You don't pick one AI strategy for sales. You pick three.**

The amount of AI you push into the customer-facing motion is inversely proportional to ACV.

Enterprise: AI makes the rep sharper. It never owns the room.
Mid-market: AI-enabled, human last. The rep still sends.
SMB: the agent owns the outreach. The human reviews and hits send.

Same company. Three different motions. One spectrum.

Swipe through the breakdown 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with bolded contrarian thesis ("You don't pick one — you pick three"). Uses Anthony's inverse-relationship framing from the source kernel verbatim. Short declarative cadence. Closes with the pointing-down emoji CTA — his signature swipe move for carousels.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 6-slide carousel mapping AI usage to deal size across enterprise, mid-market, and SMB sales motions. Cover opens the thesis, three framework slides detail each segment, a stat slide anchors the inverse-relationship rule, and a dark closing slide lands the takeaway.

params.title: The Agentic-Per-Segment Spectrum
params.body: You don't pick one AI strategy for sales. You pick three. Where you apply AI is inversely proportional to ACV.

params.title: AI sharpens the rep. Never owns the room.
params.body: Six-figure deals are still won by people. The agent preps the call, surfaces the account intel, drafts the follow-up. The rep does the deal.

params.title: AI-enabled. Human last.
params.body: Agents draft the outbound, score the intent, build the sequence. A rep reviews every touch before it goes out. Speed up, but keep the judgment loop in.

params.title: Agent owns outreach. Human hits send.
params.body: Low ACV math only works if cost per touch drops near zero. The agent runs the play. The human is the safety check, not the operator.

params.title: AI intensity scales inversely with ACV
params.body: The bigger the deal, the less AI sits between you and the buyer. The smaller the deal, the more the agent has to carry.

params.title: Agentic powers the experience. It doesn't own the room.
params.body: The agent is there to make the rep look good and make the rep sharper. It is not there to close your deal.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The Speed-to-Market AI Scorecard
posts.postText:
**Stop asking "is the AI working?" Start tracking six numbers.**

Most teams roll out agents and grade them on vibes. Six months later the bill is up, the pipeline is flat, and nobody can point to what changed.

Here's the scorecard we use:

— Meeting volume per rep
— Pipeline stage conversion
— Client response times
— Customer adoption of recommendations
— Revenue per head
— Cost utilization

If those aren't moving in 90 days, your AI isn't working. Your bill is just growing.

Save the graphic for your next QBR 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded contrarian thesis up top. Uses Anthony's plain operator vocabulary — meeting volume, conversion, revenue per head, cost utilization — straight from the source. Em-dash bullets per his formatting pattern. Closes with the save-for-later CTA + pointing-down emoji.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single-image scorecard listing the six AI performance metrics with the 90-day diagnostic line as the headline frame.

params.title: Six numbers that tell you if your AI is working
items: Meeting volume per rep
Pipeline stage conversion
Client response times
Customer adoption of recommendations
Revenue per head
Cost utilization

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Human + Agentic, not Just Agentic
posts.postText:
**"Agentic GTM" tells your team they're being replaced. "Human + Agentic GTM" tells them they're being upgraded.**

The word "plus" is doing the work.

If you frame the transformation as bodifying the org, you get fear, foot-dragging, and quiet quitting. If you frame it as augmentation, you get pull from the team — they bring you the next agent to build.

This isn't marketing nuance. It's change management.

The move from agentic-first to agentic-enabled to agentic-native only happens if your team wants to ride the curve with you.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with bolded contrarian construction ("X — not Y" inverted into a direct claim). Uses Anthony's signature "This isn't X, it's Y" pattern on the close. Plain operator vocabulary, short paragraphs, no emoji — keeps this one tight to vary from the carousel and infographic.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Hire People Better Than You, Even Into Your Own Org
posts.postText:
Talked with a public-company CRO last week who pulled off something most leaders never do: he went from spending nearly all his time on core revenue last year to spending a quarter of his time on it this year.

The unlock wasn't outsourcing. It wasn't AI. It wasn't a reorg.

He hired one VP into his own org who was better than him at the part he was trying to step back from.

That's the whole move.

Most CROs trying to redistribute their time look outside the building — a fractional, a consultant, a tool. The cleaner play is hiring complementary talent INTO the function that takes the thing further than you ever did.

It requires a posture most leaders find genuinely hard: admitting the best version of your business is one where you're not the smartest person in the room on every domain.

The ones who can sit with that get their time back. The ones who can't stay stuck in the work.
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern-from-conversation hook ("Talked with a CRO last week") in Anthony's INDUSTRY OBSERVATION / CASE STUDY hybrid mode. Uses concrete numbers from the source kernel (nearly all → a quarter). Lands on a quiet aphorism, not a CTA — varies the close so not every post ends with an emoji or sign-off.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Science Fair Strategy for New Revenue Lines
posts.postText:
Best description of new-revenue strategy I've heard from a public-company CRO this year:

"It feels like a science fair experiment."

Lots of ideas. Wide range from on-brand to a little out there. Wide range from easy to nearly impossible. More on the table than the team can deliver.

That honesty is the differentiator.

Most leaders defending a public number can't admit the next leg of growth looks messy. They package it. They make it sound inevitable. They lose the right to experiment because the narrative won't allow it.

The ones who do find the next monetization line protect time for the science fair — and tell their board that's what it is.

Defend the core. Run experiments on the edges. Don't pretend they're the same posture.
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern-recognition / INDUSTRY OBSERVATION voice with a paraphrased quote — straight out of Anthony's playbook. No emoji, no signature closer, lands on a tight three-line payoff. Varies opener (no bolded thesis here, leads with a quoted observation) and closes clean to keep the batch from feeling stamped.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Agentic Use Cases Map by Sales Motion
posts.postText:
We've been mapping where AI agents actually belong across Enterprise, Mid-market, and SMB motions — and the answer is different at every tier.

Enterprise: AI preps, human owns.
Mid-market: AI accelerates, human approves.
SMB: AI executes, human reviews.

It's a 1-pager every CRO should build before their team reinvents the wheel three times in parallel.

Swipe through 👇

#revops #aiagents
posts.voiceNotes: Uses 'We've been mapping' opener (we-framing), tight 12-20 word sentences, parallel structure for the three motions, swipe-pointer 👇, and clean hashtag bundle close. Avoids contrarian hook — frames as observation/build, not a hot take.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel mapping agentic AI use cases across three sales motions: Enterprise (AI preps, human owns), Mid-market (AI accelerates, human approves), SMB (AI executes, human reviews). Opens with a cover slide framing the map, middle slides cover each motion with concrete use cases, closes with a dark CTA slide.

params.title: Where AI Belongs by Sales Motion
params.body: A 1-page matrix every CRO should build before the team reinvents the wheel.

params.title: AI Preps. Human Owns.
params.body: Pre-meeting research, account briefs, proposal drafts, call summaries. The rep still owns every customer-facing moment.

params.title: AI Accelerates. Human Approves.
params.body: Outbound drafts, multithreading suggestions, deal-risk flags, renewal motions. Rep approves before send.

params.title: AI Executes. Human Reviews.
params.body: Outreach, lead routing, follow-ups, renewal prompts. Human reviews weekly, not per-touch.

params.title: Without It, Every Team Reinvents the Wheel
params.body: Pick the motion. Pick the use case. Pick the human checkpoint. Then deploy.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: The 25/25/25/25 CRO Calendar
posts.postText:
Saw a CRO do this last week and it's been stuck in my head.

He color-codes his calendar in 4 colors:

Blue — core revenue
Red — new monetization
Green — agentic transformation
Yellow — people

Audits it weekly. Target is roughly a quarter in each. If any color hasn't shown up in two weeks, that's the signal — not a gut check, not a guess.

Simple mechanism, hard to argue with.

#revops
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with 'Saw a CRO do this' — operator-to-operator anecdote, not contrarian. Unicode-style implicit bullets (color — label). Short paragraphs, hashtag close. Hits the kernel without preaching.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single 1080x1080 infographic showing a 4-quadrant calendar visual with each color block labeled: Blue = Core Revenue, Red = New Monetization, Green = Agentic Transformation, Yellow = People. Each block at roughly 25 percent. Headline 'The 25/25/25/25 CRO Calendar.'

params.title: The CRO Calendar Allocation
params.body: Blue: Core revenue. Red: New monetization. Green: Agentic transformation. Yellow: People. If a color is missing for two weeks, that is the audit signal.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Every Role Should Live in Gong
posts.postText:
If you work in a revenue org and you're not in Gong weekly, you're falling behind. Doesn't matter what your title is.

A few non-negotiables we recommend:

Finance — don't approve a comp plan without listening to 5 calls from the segment it covers.
RevOps — don't redesign territories without listening to 5 calls from the reps in them.
Product marketing — don't ship a new pitch without listening to 5 calls where the old one missed.
Leadership — don't sign off on a strategy shift without listening to 5 calls from the customers it affects.

The fastest way to stall out in a revenue career is to grow distant from raw customer conversations.

The fastest way to accelerate one is the opposite.
posts.voiceNotes: TACTICAL PLAYBOOK with implicit Unicode-style bullets (Role — em-dash — directive). Operator-to-operator tone, parallel structure, no emoji, no engagement bait. Clean payoff line, no hashtag close — varies the sign-off.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Selling Never Ends in Ad/Platform Businesses
posts.postText:
Pattern we keep running into when ad-supported and platform businesses copy a SaaS playbook:

In SaaS, the rep closes a contract and hands to CS or AM. The selling event has an end.

In ad and platform models, the selling never ends. Same rep keeps going — next budget cycle, the tentpole event, the new ad format, the next-quarter commitment.

If you build a SaaS-style org chart on top of an ad/platform/marketplace business, you've split the relationship the rep needs to keep selling.

The motion isn't the same. The org chart shouldn't be either.
posts.voiceNotes: INDUSTRY OBSERVATION using 'Pattern we keep running into' — matches his sample phrasing pattern. We-framing, no emoji, tight paragraphs, clean payoff landing. No hashtag close — keeps the sign-off variety.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Why Ad-Model Companies Don't Run SDR
posts.postText:
Nextdoor doesn't have an SDR layer. A handful of other ad and platform businesses we've worked with have made the same call.

The reason isn't AI. The handoff kills the relationship the senior rep needs to keep selling into the same account, cycle after cycle.

What we are seeing:

Senior strategic reps prospecting themselves, faster than they used to.
AI tooling finally making that work tolerable for people who historically hated it.
Fewer mid-funnel handoff seams to manage.

The SDR org isn't dying because of AI. In some business models it never fit in the first place. AI is just the thing that finally let the structure catch up to the motion.

🫡
posts.voiceNotes: INDUSTRY OBSERVATION with name-drop (Nextdoor) in line 1 — matches his anchor-with-real-companies hook style. Implicit bulleted observations, salute close 🫡 (his signature, used once across the batch). We-framing throughout.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Shared Priorities Map: The Forgotten Exec Exercise
posts.postText:
One of the best exec exercises I've watched a CEO run.

He didn't ask each leader for their top 5 OKRs. He asked which of their priorities were SOLO and which were SHARED with another C-suite peer.

Kiernan wants to agentify the GTM team. Great. He can't do that without Craig over in Product.

The shared priority IS the priority. Not Kiernan's wishlist. Not Craig's roadmap. The coupling between them.

Most exec teams skip this step and wonder why their OKRs feel like five parallel companies.

Swipe through for how the exercise works.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with an admiring frame about a leader he respects (a Cameron move — celebrating other operators rather than punching down). Plain operator vocabulary, no contrarian-bait. Short lines, no emojis, no hashtags. Closes with a soft swipe CTA which fits the carousel format without sounding salesy.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel teaching the Shared Priorities Map exec exercise. Cover slide names the concept. Middle slides walk through the steps: list each leader's priorities, mark each one solo or shared, name the partner exec, then make the coupling itself the tracked priority. A stat slide highlights why the exercise matters. Closing dark slide drops the punchline that the shared priority is the priority.

params.title: The Shared Priorities Map
params.body: How one CEO reframed OKRs so leaders actually had to work together.

params.title: List each leader's top priorities
params.body: Start with the usual exec OKR exercise. Every C-suite leader writes their top 3-5 for the next 12 months.

params.title: Tag each one solo or shared
params.body: For every priority, the leader marks it as something they can deliver alone or something they need a peer to unlock.

params.title: GTM and Product, coupled
items: GTM leader wants to agentify the sales team
Needs Product to ship the agent surface area
Neither side owns the outcome alone
The shared priority becomes the tracked one

params.title: Most OKRs are not solo
params.statLabel: of exec priorities require another exec to land
params.body: Most exec teams plan as five parallel companies. The Shared Priorities Map forces the dependency graph onto the page.

params.title: The shared priority is the priority
params.body: Not your wishlist. Not their roadmap. The coupling between you.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Don't Run From RevOps, Stack These 3 Experiences
posts.postText:
To the RevOps leader who wants to be a CRO one day.

The instinct is to jump out, grab an AE bag, and start carrying quota for the optics. I get it. Don't do it.

Pin-seek these three career assignments instead:

A new line of business. An international expansion. A painful re-org.

That's how you see the whole elephant. That's how you build the operational fluency the modern CRO actually needs.

The bag will be there. The reps to learn from will be there. These three assignments are the rare ones.
posts.voiceNotes: Direct address opener (To the RevOps leader who wants to...) — collegial, almost coaching. Uses his actual phrasing 'pin-seeking' and 'see the whole elephant'. No emoji, no hashtags. Lands on a quiet, confident payoff rather than a sales CTA.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A single-slide infographic listing the three career assignments a RevOps leader should pin-seek on the path to CRO: new line of business, international expansion, painful re-org. Title at the top, three numbered items, a short payoff line at the bottom about seeing the whole elephant.

params.title: 3 assignments to pin-seek before the bag
items: A new line of business — own the build from zero
An international expansion — learn the new-market muscle
A painful re-org — feel the org design tradeoffs
Then, and only then, the quota-carrying chapter

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Accountability Doesn't Bot
posts.postText:
Pattern across the buyer conversations I've been in this quarter.

You can automate the workflow. You cannot automate the accountability.

When there's a real deal on the line, buyers want to know a human is on the hook. The bigger the ACV, the more this shows up.

Fully-automated outbound at 10x volume isn't converting enterprise buyers. It's turning them off.

The agents are great at the work. The accountability still has to sit with a person.
posts.voiceNotes: INDUSTRY OBSERVATION shape — opens with 'pattern across the buyer conversations,' a credible vantage point for the Head of Customers. Teacherly, calm, not contrarian or combative. Lands on the new mental model without a CTA. No emoji.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: You Don't Need a Bag, You Need the Pressure
posts.postText:
Best career framing I've heard recently from a peer further up the ladder than me.

The common advice for an operator who wants to be a CRO is carry a bag at some point. It misses the point.

What you actually need is a stretch of years where a meaningful slice of your comp is tied to revenue. Kiernan structured his that way from his first ops role on. 30% or more of total comp, every year, tied to the top line.

He didn't take the bag for the optics. He took the pressure for the lesson.

That pressure is what teaches you how the company actually breathes. Where the leaks are. Which deals you'd kill for. Which ones you'd kill yourself.

You can get that without a quota. You can also have a quota and never feel it.

The lesson is the pressure. Not the title on the comp plan.
posts.voiceNotes: PERSONAL STORY shape — opens with 'best career framing I've heard,' which mirrors his observed PERSONAL STORY hook style (Best career advice I ever heard...). Earnest, grateful tone. Credits the peer rather than self-aggrandizing. Quiet landing line, no CTA, no emoji.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The Operator-First CRO Path
posts.postText:
Quick reframe for RevOps leaders thinking about their next 5 years.

The fastest-growing path to CRO right now isn't quota-carrying AE to sales manager to VP Sales to CRO. The more execs I get to meet in our industry, the more I see a different shape.

RevOps or SE, into an ops leader role, into a sales-comfortable executive. Some of them came out of management consulting. Many are operational first and closers second.

The modern CRO has to plan capacity, model scenarios, design comp, and yes — sit in deals. The operator-first version of that job is the version winning seats right now.

If you're in RevOps, the runway is shorter than you think. Start stacking the executive-adjacent work now.
posts.voiceNotes: SOFT EDUCATIONAL — opens with 'Quick reframe' as a calm setup line (not contrarian, not hot-take). Teacherly explanation of the new CRO archetype. Plain operator vocabulary, no hashtags, no emoji. Closes with a forward-looking nudge, not a sales CTA.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The AI Pendulum: Centralized to Wild West to Middle
posts.postText:
Most companies are now on their third AI org model in 18 months.

The pendulum swung way out to the left (one centralized AI team controls everything), then way out to the right (let every team go rogue), and is now settling somewhere in the middle.

The middle is the right answer. The first two phases were the tax to get there.

Built a quick visual on the three phases and what to skip. Swipe through.
posts.voiceNotes: Uses the 'pattern across companies' observation hook he favors, lands on a reframe (the middle isn't a compromise, it's the answer), and avoids hot-take labels. Closes with a clean carousel pointer instead of a question-bait CTA.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel walking through three phases of AI org design (centralized, wild west, federated-with-guardrails), what each phase costs, and what to skip if you're starting fresh today.

params.title: The AI Pendulum
params.body: Centralized team. Then everyone goes rogue. Then guardrails in the middle. Most companies are on their third model in 18 months.

params.title: Centralized AI Team
params.body: One team owns every tool, every prompt, every workflow. Safe, slow, and the line of process owners outside their door gets long fast.

params.title: Wild West
params.body: Let every team go pick their own tools. Curiosity spikes. So does the credit card bill. The $20k surprise invoice usually shows up around month four.

params.title: Guardrails in the Middle
params.body: Centralized cost and tool guardrails. Process owners keep final say. Curiosity stays alive. The bill stops surprising anyone.

params.title: Day-One Setup
items: Set a per-team monthly AI spend cap before anyone signs up for anything
Name a process owner for every workflow you want AI to touch
Approve a short list of tools, leave room for two experiments per team
Review the bill and the wins together, monthly, in the same meeting

params.title: Pay the curiosity tax. Skip the chaos tax.
params.body: The middle was always the answer. You don't have to live through the first two phases to earn it.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The New Function Question for AI
posts.postText:
The question every leadership team is wrestling with right now:

Do you spin up a new department to drive AI transformation, or let the existing functions own it?

It's not a values question. It's a structure question.

- Functional org? Let the functions own it.
- GM or line-of-business org? Let the GMs own it.
- Weak data foundation? You need a centralized transformation group, at least until the plumbing is fixed.

Your org structure picks the answer for you. Most teams just haven't asked the question that way yet.
posts.voiceNotes: Reframe hook in the second line ('It's not a values question. It's a structure question.'), parallel bullets in his preferred grammatical structure, no emoji, clean declarative landing. Avoids the salute close.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A single-image decision guide showing three org structures (functional, GM/line-of-business, weak-data) and the matching answer for where AI transformation should live.

params.title: Your Org Structure Picks the Answer
items: Functional org -> let the functions own their processes
GM / line-of-business org -> let the GMs own the P&L of it
Weak data foundation -> centralized transformation team, until the plumbing is fixed
Strong data + clear process owners -> federated, with cost and tool guardrails
No clear owner anywhere -> that's your real problem, not the org chart

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The Curiosity Tax
posts.postText:
When you tell your team "go experiment with AI," you are choosing to pay a tax.

Surprise bills. Redundant tools. Subscriptions nobody approved. People integrating things they probably shouldn't be integrating.

That's the cost. Most leaders try to skip it and end up with a frozen org instead.

The tax is the learning. It's the only way the company finds out which tools actually move work, which workflows break, and which process owners have real instincts vs. which ones are just loud.

Pay the curiosity tax on purpose. Cap it, don't kill it.
posts.voiceNotes: Contrarian reframe in the second beat (the tax IS the learning), parallel concrete examples in his bullet rhythm, lands on a one-line prescription. No emoji, no question bait, no hashtag bundle.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Avoid the Big Mistakes, Not Every Mistake
posts.postText:
I'm paranoid about how much my team spends on AI tools. So I built the budget the wrong way for six months.

The standard isn't spend zero. It isn't even show ROI. It's avoid the catastrophic mistakes.

Most teams over-engineer ROI tracking and under-engineer guardrails against the $100k whoopsie. Flip it.

What we actually do now:

1. Hard per-seat and per-tool monthly caps. Auto-pause at the cap, not a warning email.
2. A short list of approved data sources any tool can touch. Nothing else gets connected without a second pair of eyes.
3. One person owns the bill end-to-end. Not finance. The function leader using the tool.
4. A monthly 20-minute review: biggest spend, biggest win, biggest near-miss. That's it.
5. ROI tracking gets one quarter to prove itself before we kill it. Most ROI dashboards die. That's fine.

Set a low floor on losses. Don't set a high bar on wins. The wins show up when you stop strangling the experiments.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with a small admission instead of a contrarian label, then pivots to the reframe and a numbered SOP — his TACTICAL PLAYBOOK shape. Uses 'flip it' and 'that's it' punch sentences. Clean declarative close, no CTA.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The Inbound Quality Cliff
posts.postText:
Pattern I'm seeing on both sides of the GTM tool market right now:

Inbound volume is up roughly 10x in the last year.
Inbound quality is off a cliff.

I'm a buyer for sales tools, training programs, RevOps tools. The volume hitting my inbox has multiplied. Almost none of it is good. Most of it is turning me off the entire category.

Which is the part founders selling AI to GTM teams are not internalizing yet:

The people you most want to reach — operating CROs and Heads of GTM evaluating sales tools — are actively turned off by automated outreach right now. The same motion that got you to your first ten customers is now your biggest negative signal to the next hundred.

Every founder selling AI to GTM teams is competing in a market they themselves are sick of buying in.

Worth sitting with that for a second before you turn the sequencer back on.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry observation hook ('Pattern I'm seeing'), parallel structure on the two cliffs, lands with a meta-reframe rather than a CTA. No emoji, no hashtag bundle, no salute. Uses his 'worth sitting with that' soft close.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: From Agentic-First to Agentic-Enabled to Agentic-Native
posts.postText:
Most teams think they're agentic-native because they have a Claude tab open.

They're actually still in stage one.

We've been mapping a maturity curve internally at LeanScale Labs to figure out which lever we pull next quarter. Three stages, very different optimization problems at each one.

If your AI roadmap doesn't say which stage you're at, you're probably overspending on the wrong layer.

Swipe through the curve. Happy to share the diagnostic we use with clients if anyone wants it.
posts.voiceNotes: Artifact-led opener (the curve itself is the artifact), declarative sentences, builder-pragmatic register. Soft practitioner CTA at the close ("happy to share"). Avoids contrarian-bait phrasing. References LeanScale Labs naturally.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel walking through a 3-stage AI maturity curve: agentic-first, agentic-enabled, agentic-native. Opens with a cover slide, three framework slides (one per stage with a number, definition, and what to optimize), closes with a dark slide on why knowing the stage matters.

params.title: Agentic-first to agentic-enabled to agentic-native
params.body: Three stages. Most companies confuse stage one for stage three. Knowing which one you're at changes what you optimize for next quarter.

params.title: Agentic-first
params.body: Experiments. Side projects. A few people running Claude in tabs. Optimize for: signal on what works, not productivity.

params.title: Agentic-enabled
params.body: Real workflows get assisted. Reps, marketers, ops people use agents in their day jobs. Optimize for: training, guardrails, data quality.

params.title: Agentic-native
params.body: The org couldn't function without agents in the loop. Optimize for: org design, monetization, and what only humans should still touch.

params.title: Stage one with a stage three budget
params.body: Most teams skip the middle. They buy enterprise-grade tooling for hobbyist-grade workflows. Map your stage first, then spend.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The Data Moat Becomes a Monetization Asset
posts.postText:
If you have a real first-party data moat, you almost certainly have un-shipped revenue sitting inside it.

Nextdoor is a clean example. 110 million verified neighbors. One in three U.S. homes. The product graph isn't just a defensibility story anymore. It's a new revenue line, on platform and off.

What caught my attention listening to their team: they aren't talking about selling data. They're talking about carefully designing models that monetize the graph itself.

Most companies have less data leverage than they think. The ones with a real moat tend to under-monetize it for years before someone walks in and runs the math.

One visual on what the shift looks like.
posts.voiceNotes: Show-don't-shout opener (leads with the assertion + the proof point). Names a specific company and specific numbers. Plain-spoken, builder-pragmatic. Closes by pointing to the visual rather than a CTA, which matches Jake's artifact-led pattern. No emoji. No hashtags.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single 1080x1080 infographic positioned as a stat callout with supporting body copy: 110M verified neighbors as the hero stat, with a short list of the three monetization vectors a real data moat unlocks (on-platform models, off-platform partnerships, derivative products).

params.title: The graph is the asset
params.body: On-platform models, off-platform partnerships, derivative products — three monetization vectors most data-rich companies leave on the table for years.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The $20k Surprise Bill
posts.postText:
The first sign your AI experimentation is real isn't a productivity dashboard.

It's an invoice nobody expected.

One team I heard about recently: a single person running agents in the background for a month. Bill came in at $20,000. IT noticed before product did.

The reflex is to shut it down. The right move is the opposite.

A $20k surprise bill is a leading indicator. It means somebody on your team has crossed from "playing with the tool" into "actually getting work done with it" — they probably just don't have the cost intuition yet to know how expensive that work is.

What we do at LeanScale when we see this:

- Find the person. Don't punish them.
- Map what they were trying to do.
- Put usage guardrails in (budgets, alerting, model routing).
- Bring them in as the internal champion.

The bill is feedback. Treat it like product telemetry, not a fire.
posts.voiceNotes: TACTICAL PLAYBOOK underneath a case study framing. Opens with a quiet reframe rather than a contrarian bait. Specific number ($20k), specific actor (IT noticed first), short bulleted action list — Jake's stack-component bullet style. Soft, declarative landing line.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Your CRM Quality Is Now Your AI Ceiling
posts.postText:
Most companies are about to discover that the limit on their AI productivity isn't model choice.

It's their data foundation.

The pattern I keep seeing: teams who skipped the boring Salesforce-to-warehouse work in 2023 and 2024 are now hitting a ceiling on what their AI tools can actually do for them. The agent is only ever as good as the table it queries.

A story from Nextdoor's team that stuck with me. They crawled through a painful Salesforce integration before the AI moment. Cleaned the CRM, wired it to Databricks, made the pipes boring and reliable. None of it looked like AI work at the time.

Now non-data-scientists on their team are running like data scientists. Tools like Databricks and Salesforce just talk to whatever agent is sitting on top.

That's the lesson. The crawl in 2024 unlocked the run in 2026.

If you skipped the crawl, the ceiling is real. And it's about to feel low.
posts.voiceNotes: SOFT EDUCATIONAL teaching one concept (data foundation = AI ceiling). Uses Jake's preferred construction "the pattern I keep seeing". Names specific platforms (Salesforce, Databricks) without hype. Quiet, declarative landing. No CTA. No emoji.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Be Slower on AI in New Product Lines
posts.postText:
Counterintuitive thing we're starting to see inside LeanScale Labs.

The place to move FAST with agents is your mature, well-understood offerings. The place to move SLOW is brand-new product lines and lower-ACV experiments.

Most teams have this inverted. They're cautious where they should be aggressive, and aggressive where they should be cautious.

The logic:

- Mature products have a known shape. Agents amplify what you already understand.
- New products need nuanced customer feedback. The kind you only get from talking to a human who's three weeks into using the thing.
- Surveys won't surface it. An agent definitely won't.

For newer monetization motions, the fastest path to learning is still a 30-minute call with the customer.

We've been pushing this internally as a sequencing rule for our own LeanScale Labs experiments. Move fast on AI in the parts of the business that are already known. Move slow on AI in the parts you're still discovering.
posts.voiceNotes: INDUSTRY OBSERVATION grounded in LeanScale Labs as the vantage point — that earns Jake the right to claim the pattern. Sequencing-rule structure matches his builder voice. Short bulleted body, declarative landing, no CTA. Reframes "counterintuitive" without using contrarian-bait phrasing like "unpopular opinion".
